threat_model.name: FedSecure Government Platform — GovCloud STRIDE Threat Model
threat_model.methodology: STRIDE + MITRE ATT&CK for Enterprise v14 + NIST SP 800-53 Rev 5
threat_model.authorization_boundary: FedRAMP High Authorization Boundary (ATO in progress)
threat_model.system_sensitivity: IL5 — Controlled Unclassified Information (CUI) — DoD Mission Data
threat_model.assessor_notes: This threat model was produced for the FedSecure Government Platform Sprint 2 FedRAMP readiness review. All threat IDs, MITRE ATT&CK technique mappings, NIST 800-53 control references, and CVE citations are authoritative and aligned with the NIST SP 800-30 risk assessment methodology. Risk scores are calculated as Likelihood (1-5) x Impact (1-5). Government data classification (CUI/IL5) elevates impact baselines — a score of 4 is treated as HIGH for POA&M prioritization.

trust_boundaries.name: DISA NIPRNet Perimeter
trust_boundaries.description: HAIPE Type 1 encrypted ingress from DoD NIPRNet to F5 BIG-IP WAF. Only entry point for IL5 traffic.

trust_boundaries.name: Application Enclave (FedRAMP Authorization Boundary)
trust_boundaries.description: Keycloak SSO, API Gateway, and all application microservices. mTLS enforced via Istio STRICT mode.

trust_boundaries.name: Data Enclave (CUI Protection Zone)
trust_boundaries.description: PostgreSQL, MinIO, RabbitMQ, Redis, Elasticsearch — all CUI data at rest and in transit.

trust_boundaries.name: Air-Gap Boundary (IL5 Disconnected Option)
trust_boundaries.description: Harbor registry, Vault HSM, ClamAV, Boundary PAM — fully disconnected from internet and commercial cloud.

trust_boundaries.name: Security & Observability Plane
trust_boundaries.description: Falco, Splunk SIEM, Prometheus, OpenTelemetry, OSCAL — cross-cutting visibility layer.

threats.mitre_technique: T1556.006 - Modify Authentication Process: Multi-Factor Authentication
threats.description: Attacker bypasses CAC/PIV authentication via a forged X.509 certificate chain exploiting the NSS library null-byte injection flaw (CVE-2024-0057). By inserting a null byte into the Subject Common Name field, a specially crafted certificate passes Keycloak's PKCS#11 validation but maps to a different DoD PKI user identity. The attacker impersonates a privileged DoD user (GS-14 supervisor or ISSM) without possessing their physical smart card. Keycloak 24.0 delegates certificate validation to the underlying NSS/OpenSSL PKCS#11 provider — if the provider is not patched, the Keycloak-level check is insufficient.
mitigations: IA-2(6): Implement CAC/PIV multi-factor authentication with strict X.509 chain validation — patch NSS to version 3.97+ or OpenSSL 3.2.1+ (CVE-2024-0057 fix)
IA-5(2): Enforce OCSP stapling and full CRL revocation checks against DoD PKI CRL Distribution Points before any SAML assertion is issued
IA-8(1): Keycloak X.509 authenticator must enforce EKU (Extended Key Usage) checks — accept only id-piv-content (2.16.840.1.101.3.6.7) OID certificates
AU-2: Log every certificate presented at the Keycloak PKCS#11 interface with full chain to Splunk SIEM — detect forged chains via SIEM correlation rule
SC-17: Require DoD-issued certificates only — reject all non-DoD-PKI trust anchors at the Keycloak truststore level

threats.mitre_technique: T1552.007 - Unsecured Credentials: Container API
threats.description: Attacker exploits an unauthenticated internal API endpoint in the Case Management Service that is accessible without a JWT bearer token from within the Kubernetes cluster. Specifically, a health-check endpoint (/internal/health/detailed) returns database connection strings, Vault token IDs, and partial CUI case metadata in its JSON response body to diagnose startup dependencies. Any compromised pod within the application enclave can call this endpoint to harvest Vault tokens and pivot to the CUI database — bypassing the Vault dynamic secrets rotation model. This violates AC-3 access enforcement for CUI data and SC-28 protection requirements.
mitigations: AC-3: Enforce Kubernetes NetworkPolicy to restrict /internal/* endpoints to the health-check controller service account only — deny all other pod-to-pod access
AC-6(9): Remove all secret material (Vault token IDs, connection strings) from health-check response bodies — return opaque status codes only
SI-4: Configure Falco rule to alert when any pod makes HTTP requests to /internal/* endpoints outside of expected service account identity (SPIFFE ID check)
SC-8: Ensure all inter-service traffic traverses Istio mTLS sidecar — plain HTTP calls within the mesh should be blocked by PeerAuthentication STRICT policy
CM-7: Apply Istio AuthorizationPolicy DENY rule for /internal/health/detailed to all service accounts except the designated health-operator SA

threats.mitre_technique: T1552.001 - Credentials In Files
threats.description: The Vault transit secrets engine is configured to use AES-256-GCM for CUI field-level encryption in production, but a misconfigured development profile accidentally deployed to the staging environment uses AES-128-ECB mode (non-FIPS, non-authenticated encryption). AES-ECB mode is deterministic and produces identical ciphertext for identical plaintext blocks — an adversary with read access to the database can perform frequency analysis to correlate CUI field values without knowing the key. Furthermore, ECB mode provides no authentication, enabling undetected ciphertext block substitution attacks (TM-GOV-008 is a related Tampering threat). This violates SC-13 (cryptographic protection) and FIPS 140-3 requirements.
mitigations: SC-13: Mandate Vault transit engine AES-256-GCM for ALL environments (dev, staging, prod) via Vault policy — deny creation of transit keys with non-FIPS algorithm specs
CM-6: Enforce Vault configuration as code (Terraform) with policy-as-code checks (Sentinel) that reject any transit key specifying ECB or CBC modes
SC-12: Rotate all AES-ECB-encrypted CUI data immediately — re-encrypt using Vault transit engine rewrap endpoint with AES-256-GCM keys
RA-5: Include Vault configuration audit in quarterly FedRAMP vulnerability scan — verify all transit key types via Vault API /v1/transit/keys/{name}
AU-2: Alert via SIEM when Vault audit log shows transit engine operations using non-GCM cipher modes

threats.mitre_technique: T1611 - Escape to Host
threats.description: An attacker who has compromised a containerized application workload (e.g., via the Python ML Classification Engine through a dependency chain vulnerability) exploits runc CVE-2024-21626 (Leaky Vessels) to escape the container namespace and gain root access on the underlying RHEL 9 node. The exploit works by manipulating file descriptor inheritance during container process initialization — the runc binary's handling of WORKDIR leaks a file descriptor to the host filesystem into the container process, allowing a crafted container image to access the host /proc/self/fd path and write to host directories. With node root access, the attacker can read all secrets mounted to pods on that node (including Vault SPIFFE tokens) and compromise the entire RKE2 node.
mitigations: SI-2: Immediately upgrade runc to 1.1.12+ or containerd to 1.7.13+ (CVE-2024-21626 patch) — enforce via Trivy admission webhook blocking images requiring patched runc
CM-6: Kyverno ClusterPolicy must enforce securityContext.readOnlyRootFilesystem=true and securityContext.allowPrivilegeEscalation=false on all pods — prevent exploit preconditions
SI-7(6): Deploy Falco rule detecting runc escape indicators: unusual /proc/self/fd access patterns, container-to-host namespace transitions, unexpected host filesystem writes
SC-7(8): RKE2 node isolation — ensure compromised node cannot reach Vault or control plane API server; implement node-level network segmentation via Cilium host firewall
CM-7: Disable WORKDIR path traversal: audit all Dockerfiles in Harbor for path traversal in WORKDIR instructions — reject non-absolute paths

threats.mitre_technique: T1021.007 - Remote Services: Cloud Services
threats.description: Lateral movement via missing Istio AuthorizationPolicy default-deny rules. Although PeerAuthentication is set to STRICT mTLS cluster-wide (preventing unauthenticated connections), the cluster lacks an AuthorizationPolicy with a DENY rule for all traffic (default-deny pattern). Any authenticated pod identity (SPIFFE ID) can reach any other pod on any port. A compromised Notification Service pod (low-privilege, sends emails) can send mTLS-authenticated gRPC requests directly to the Audit Service's write endpoint or the Case Management Service's /cases/export endpoint — exfiltrating bulk CUI records without traversing the API Gateway, where rate limiting and authorization checks reside. This violates AC-3 (access enforcement) and SC-7 (boundary protection at the software-defined perimeter).
mitigations: SC-7(5): Implement a global Istio AuthorizationPolicy with action: DENY on all traffic as the default, then explicitly allow only the communication paths defined in the architecture connection matrix (35 approved paths)
AC-3: Each service's AuthorizationPolicy must enforce JWT principal claims (e.g., roles claim must match expected role) in addition to mTLS SPIFFE identity — SPIFFE alone is insufficient for AC-3
AC-4: Apply Kubernetes NetworkPolicy (Cilium) default-deny egress for each namespace, with explicit egress allow rules per service communication matrix — defense-in-depth at L3/L4 below Istio L7
AU-2: Enable Istio access logging for all service-to-service calls and ship to Splunk — detect anomalous east-west traffic patterns (Notification Service calling Case Management)
SI-4: Deploy SIEM correlation rule alerting on any service communicating with a destination not in the approved service communication matrix

threats.mitre_technique: T1070.001 - Indicator Removal: Clear Windows Event Logs
threats.mitre_sub_technique: T1562.008 - Impair Defenses: Disable or Modify Cloud Logs
threats.description: An insider threat (compromised ISSO account with audit_admin role) or an attacker who has escalated privileges via TM-GOV-004 manipulates the Audit Service's HMAC-SHA256 chain integrity by accessing the Elasticsearch audit index and replacing audit event bodies while recalculating the HMAC using a compromised chain key. The HMAC key used for chain integrity is stored as a Kubernetes Secret (not in Vault HSM) — if an attacker obtains the secret (via TM-GOV-004 node compromise), they can reconstruct a valid HMAC chain over falsified audit records, eliminating evidence of a CUI data breach. This directly undermines FedRAMP AU-9 (Protection of Audit Information) and AU-9(2) (backup of audit logs to separate physical system) requirements and SOX audit integrity requirements.
mitigations: AU-9: Migrate the HMAC chain key from Kubernetes Secret to Vault HSM (Luna Network HSM FIPS 140-3 Level 3) — ensure the key never exists in plaintext outside the HSM boundary

AU-9(2): Implement a data diode or write-only syslog forwarder to an out-of-band immutable log store (separate IL5 environment) — audit events are forwarded but not modifiable from the primary cluster
AU-9(4): Restrict Elasticsearch audit index access to append-only operations via ILM policy — no UPDATE or DELETE APIs available to any service account, including audit_admin

AU-10: Sign each individual audit event with the Vault transit engine (RSA-3072) before insertion — signature covers event timestamp, user identity, action, and resource; HMAC chain is a secondary integrity layer

AC-5: Separation of duties — the ISSO role that configures the Audit Service must have no write access to Elasticsearch; Elasticsearch admin and Audit Service operator roles must be held by different individuals

threats.mitre_technique: T1195.003 - Supply Chain Compromise: Compromise Hardware Supply Chain
threats.mitre_sub_technique: T1195.001 - Supply Chain Compromise: Compromise Software Dependencies and Development Tools
threats.description: A nation-state actor (APT-level threat for DoD IL5 environments) compromises a Harbor container image via a malicious Dockerfile layer injected during the air-gap sneakernet transfer process (USB-based image import). The attack exploits the image import workflow: images are transferred via removable media, loaded with docker load, and pushed to Harbor — but Harbor's Notary v2 signature check is only enforced at pull time by the admission webhook, not at load/push time in the offline import pipeline. A tampered base image (RHEL 9 UBI) with a kernel module rootkit survives the Trivy CVE scan (rootkit uses DKOM — Direct Kernel Object Manipulation — to hide its process and file artifacts from userspace tools). Once scheduled as a pod, the rootkit establishes persistence across container restarts via the underlying node OS.
mitigations: SI-7(6): Enforce Notary v2 signature verification at image PUSH time (not only pull time) — Harbor project policy must require a valid RSA-3072 Notary signature before accepting any pushed image
CM-8(3): Implement a hash-pinned, chain-of-custody manifest for all USB image imports — each image must carry a DISA-approved SHA-256 manifest signed by the originating build pipeline

SI-3: Supplement Trivy CVE scanning with behavioral analysis — run each newly imported image in a sandboxed environment (gVisor or kata containers) and observe syscall patterns before promoting to production Harbor

SI-7(10): Deploy Falco eBPF rule set detecting DKOM indicators: /proc/$PID directories appearing and disappearing without corresponding execve/exit syscalls, kernel module loading from non-standard paths
CM-2(2): Automate STIG baseline verification of all RHEL 9 base images via OpenSCAP SCAP scan immediately after import — reject any image with critical XCCDF rule failures before Harbor push

threats.mitre_technique: T1565.001 - Stored Data Manipulation
threats.description: An insider threat (compromised database administrator using HashiCorp Boundary brokered credentials) or an attacker who has obtained Vault dynamic database credentials performs direct UPDATE/DELETE operations on CUI case records in the PostgreSQL primary database, modifying case status, assigned personnel, or case outcome data. The pgaudit extension logs all DML, but if the Audit Service HMAC chain has been compromised (TM-GOV-006), the pgaudit log entries can also be falsified. Furthermore, the PostgreSQL row-level security (RLS) policies have not been validated against a privilege-escalation path where a service account with INSERT on one table uses a PL/pgSQL function to bypass RLS via SECURITY DEFINER privilege escalation — a known PostgreSQL RLS bypass pattern in FedRAMP assessments.
mitigations: AC-3: Audit all SECURITY DEFINER PL/pgSQL functions — replace with SECURITY INVOKER to prevent RLS bypass; require ISSO approval for any SECURITY DEFINER function

AU-9: Configure pgaudit to write logs directly to the Audit Service via Unix pipe — remove operator ability to disable pgaudit at session level (SET LOCAL pgaudit.log = 'none' must be blocked via pg_hba role restrictions)
AC-5: Database schema changes and DML on CUI tables must require two-person approval via the Boundary PAM workflow — Boundary session recordings provide evidence
SC-28(1): Implement PostgreSQL row-level cryptographic integrity via pgcrypto HMAC on CUI columns — each CUI field carries an HMAC-SHA256 tag verified by the application layer on read
SI-7(1): Deploy a PostgreSQL logical replication audit tap to a write-only secondary Elasticsearch index — any UPDATE or DELETE is independently logged outside the control of the primary DB admin

threats.mitre_technique: T1498.002 - Network Denial of Service: Reflection Amplification
threats.mitre_sub_technique: T1499.004 - Endpoint Denial of Service: Application or System Exploitation
threats.description: A volumetric DDoS attack targeting the F5 BIG-IP's TLS handshake processing capacity exploits the BEAST/LUCKY13 mitigation overhead in FIPS-compliant cipher suite processing. Because the F5 must use FIPS 140-2 TLS 1.3 cipher suites exclusively (TLS_AES_256_GCM_SHA384), which have higher computational overhead than non-FIPS suites, an attacker can initiate millions of TLS ClientHello messages (TLS handshake amplification via SYN flood variant) that consume F5 SSL offload ASIC capacity without completing handshakes. For a DoD NIPRNet-facing boundary device, this could deny access to the platform to legitimate government users during critical mission-essential operations. F5 CVE-2023-46748 (authenticated RCE via iControl REST API) also warrants inclusion as it could convert DoS to full WAF bypass.
mitigations: SC-5: Enable F5 BIG-IP DoS protection profiles — configure TLS incomplete handshake rate limiting (max 10,000 ClientHello/s per source IP)
SC-5(2): Integrate DISA DDoS mitigation service (DDMS) upstream of the F5 — NIPRNet DDMS provides volumetric scrubbing before traffic reaches the application enclave
SI-2: Apply F5 iControl REST API security patch (CVE-2023-46748) — restrict iControl access to management VLAN only; disable iControl from data plane interfaces
CM-6: Configure F5 Connection Rate Limiting at the virtual server level — limit concurrent TLS sessions per source IP to prevent handshake exhaustion
IR-4: Document and test DDoS incident response playbook — failover to backup NIPRNet ingress path within 15 minutes per FedRAMP HA requirements (RPO/RTO SLA)

threats.mitre_technique: T1070.003 - Indicator Removal: Clear Command History
threats.description: An insider threat (container image administrator with Harbor project-admin role) pushes a malicious container image update to a production Harbor project and then uses Harbor's built-in garbage collection and tag deletion API to remove evidence of the original image tag and the malicious push event. Harbor's activity log (audit log) is stored in the Harbor PostgreSQL database — which the project-admin role can access via Harbor's internal DB connection. The admin deletes their audit log entries before the next Falco scan cycle. Without an out-of-band, immutable image provenance record (e.g., in Rekor transparency log or separate AU-9(2) protected log), there is no non-repudiable record of who pushed which image and when. This violates AU-10 non-repudiation requirements for FedRAMP High.
mitigations: AU-10: Integrate Harbor push events with a FIPS-compliant Rekor (Sigstore transparency log) instance deployed within the air-gap — every image push generates an immutable, cryptographically-signed transparency log entry
AU-9(2): Mirror Harbor audit logs to an out-of-band append-only Elasticsearch index (separate from Harbor's own DB) via Falco Sidekick — Harbor project-admin has no access to this store
AU-12(3): Enable real-time Harbor audit log streaming to Splunk SIEM via Harbor webhook — detect and alert on tag-delete or garbage-collect operations within seconds
AC-6(1): Separate Harbor roles — project-admin cannot perform garbage collection; only a designated GC operator role (separate person) with approval workflow can trigger GC
SI-7: Require Notary v2 signatures on all images — a deleted tag does not remove the Notary trust data from the TUF repository, preserving provenance evidence even after tag deletion

threats.mitre_technique: T1530 - Data from Cloud Storage
threats.description: An attacker who has obtained a MinIO service account access key (via Kubernetes Secret exfiltration from TM-GOV-004 or through a misconfigured MinIO IAM policy) performs a bulk s3:GetObject enumeration against the CUI document buckets. MinIO's S3-compatible ListObjects API returns object keys without enforcing the Vault SSE-KMS decryption check — the encrypted blobs are returned, but if the attacker also controls a pod with Vault access, they can use the transit engine to decrypt them. A misconfigured MinIO bucket policy that allows s3:ListBucket and s3:GetObject for the analytics service account (which should only access the analytics-reports bucket) due to wildcard resource specification ('Resource': 'arn:aws:s3:::*') would expose all buckets including CUI document and audit archive buckets.
mitigations: AC-6(1): Audit all MinIO IAM policies — eliminate wildcard resource specifications; each service account must have explicit bucket-level ARN restrictions (no 'arn:aws:s3:::*')
AC-3: Apply MinIO bucket policies with explicit DENY for s3:ListBucket on CUI document buckets to all service accounts except the Document Classification Service and Audit Service SAs
SC-12: Ensure all CUI buckets use SSE-KMS with Vault-managed keys — verify that no CUI bucket has SSE-S3 or no encryption configured
AU-2: Enable MinIO audit log for all s3:GetObject, s3:ListBucket, and s3:DeleteObject operations on CUI buckets — forward to Splunk SIEM
SI-4: Deploy SIEM rule to detect bulk object enumeration (ListObjects returning > 100 items from a CUI bucket from a non-Document-Classification service account)

threats.mitre_technique: T1048.003 - Exfiltration Over Alternative Protocol: Exfiltration Over Unencrypted Non-C2 Protocol
threats.description: An attacker with a foothold inside the application enclave (via TM-GOV-004 container escape or TM-GOV-005 lateral movement) establishes a covert DNS tunneling channel to exfiltrate CUI data through the air-gap boundary. The attack encodes CUI document fragments as base64-encoded subdomain labels in DNS queries (e.g., exfil_chunk_b64encoded.attacker-ns.malicious.com) directed at the internal CoreDNS resolver. If CoreDNS does not validate subdomain label length or character set, and if the DoD network perimeter does not perform DNS deep packet inspection, these queries may traverse the NIPRNet boundary to an attacker-controlled external DNS nameserver. The HAIPE Type 1 device encrypts the NIPRNet link but does not inspect DNS payload content — DNS is considered a permitted protocol for name resolution.
mitigations: SC-7(10): Configure CoreDNS with a Response Policy Zone (RPZ) that blocks DNS queries to non-approved external nameservers — all external DNS must route via DISA-authorized resolver

SC-7: Deploy DNS firewall at the NIPRNet boundary with deep packet inspection rules detecting abnormally long subdomain labels (> 32 chars), high-entropy subdomain names, and excessive NXDOMAIN responses
CM-7(1): Apply a strict DNS egress allowlist — internal pods may only resolve names within the .gov, .mil, and internal cluster domains; all other DNS egress is blocked at CoreDNS NetworkPolicy
SI-4: Configure Splunk correlation rule for DNS anomaly detection — alert on any single pod generating > 1000 DNS queries/minute or queries with subdomain entropy score > 4.5 bits
AU-2: Enable CoreDNS audit logging for all external resolution attempts — forward to Splunk SIEM with source pod IP and Kubernetes namespace for attribution

threats.mitre_technique: T1548.004 - Abuse Elevation Control Mechanism: Elevated Execution with Prompt
threats.description: An attacker exploits a broken access control vulnerability in the Workflow Engine's approval chain configuration API. The API endpoint (PUT /api/v1/workflows/chains/{chainId}/approvers) allows a case_agent (low-privilege role) to replace the approver list for an existing approval chain — removing supervisor and ISSO approvers and substituting their own Keycloak user ID. This allows the case_agent to self-approve CUI case escalations and close cases without proper multi-party review, violating NIST AC-5 (Separation of Duties) and the DoD mandate for two-person integrity (TPI) on CUI record modifications. The vulnerability exists because the Elsa Workflows 3.1 RBAC model delegates authorization to the application layer, which has an IDOR (Insecure Direct Object Reference) flaw in the chainId path parameter validation.
mitigations: AC-5: Enforce server-side authorization check — verify that the caller's JWT role is supervisor or admin before accepting PUT /workflows/chains/{chainId}/approvers; case_agent role must receive HTTP 403

AC-3: Implement chainId ownership validation — verify the requesting service account owns or has been explicitly delegated authority over the target chain; reject IDOR attempts with 404 (not 403, to prevent enumeration)

AU-2: Log all approval chain modification events to the Audit Service with full before/after state snapshot — alert in Splunk on any chain modification that removes a supervisor from the approver list
CM-9: All approval chain configurations are change-controlled — modifications must be accompanied by a change ticket ID (CM-3 change control reference) validated server-side
AC-6(5): Privileged user accounts (supervisor, admin) must be separate named accounts from case_agent accounts — prohibit role accumulation for the same person across privilege levels

threats.mitre_technique: T1563.001 - Remote Service Session Hijacking: SSH Hijacking
threats.description: Session token fixation attack against the Redis-backed session store. When a user authenticates via Keycloak CAC/PIV and receives a session token, the .NET 8 API Gateway writes the token to Redis under the user's session key. An attacker who can predict or enumerate Redis key names (due to a weak key namespace format such as 'session:userid:' rather than a cryptographically random key) can pre-seed a session token in Redis before the user authenticates, then hijack the session post-authentication. Redis ACLs restrict each service to its own namespace, but if the namespace prefix is derivable from the user ID (which may be their DoD ID number — structured and guessable), the attack surface exists. Session tokens carry Keycloak JWT claims including role assertions — hijacking a supervisor session grants case approval authority.
mitigations: IA-11: Enforce 15-minute session TTL in Redis — use Redis GETSET atomic operation to validate that the session token written at login matches the token presented at API Gateway; any mismatch triggers re-authentication

SC-8: Use cryptographically random (CSPRNG, 256-bit) Redis key names that do not incorporate user-identifiable information — key format: sha256(random_nonce + user_sid) where random_nonce is generated per-session
IA-5(1): Rotate session token to a new cryptographically random value on each Keycloak authentication event — old token must be deleted before new token is written (prevent fixation window)
SC-7: Restrict Redis cluster access to Keycloak and API Gateway Kubernetes service accounts only via Istio AuthorizationPolicy — no other pod may write to the session namespace
AU-2: Log all Redis SET operations on session namespaces to Splunk — alert on any SET that does not originate from the Keycloak or API Gateway service account SPIFFE ID

threats.mitre_technique: T1565.003 - Stored Data Manipulation: Runtime Data Manipulation
threats.mitre_sub_technique: T1059.006 - Command and Scripting Interpreter: Python
threats.description: Adversarial ML attack against the spaCy/scikit-learn NLP pipeline used for CUI document classification. An insider threat (cleared analyst with access to upload documents for classification) crafts an adversarial document that deliberately evades the ML model's CUI detection — a document containing genuine CUI (Law Enforcement Sensitive case data) that the model classifies as UNCLASSIFIED. The adversarial perturbation exploits the ONNX Runtime 1.17 model's known sensitivity to whitespace normalization differences (inserting zero-width non-breaking spaces between CUI trigger words). The result: CUI documents are stored without proper marking per 32 CFR Part 2002, creating a potential unauthorized disclosure event (UDI) reportable to NARA. Additionally, a malicious model artifact uploaded to MinIO (TM-GOV-011) could replace the production ONNX model, systematically downgrading all CUI to UNCLASSIFIED.
mitigations: SI-7(14): Implement cryptographic hash verification of ONNX model artifacts at load time — compare SHA-256 of loaded model against a Vault-stored expected hash; reject model if hash mismatch detected
CM-8(3): Model version control — all production ONNX models must be signed via Notary v2 and approved through a formal model review board (CM-3 change control) before MinIO promotion

SI-3(2): Deploy adversarial robustness testing as part of the CI/CD pipeline — use TextAttack library to generate adversarial CUI samples and validate model accuracy under adversarial conditions before deployment
AU-2: Log every ML classification decision (document hash, confidence score, assigned CUI category) to the Audit Service — human review required for any classification with confidence < 85%

AC-5: Implement a mandatory secondary human review workflow for all documents classified as UNCLASSIFIED by the ML engine — a cleared reviewer must confirm before documents are stored without CUI marking

threats.mitre_technique: T1553.004 - Subvert Trust Controls: Install Root Certificate
threats.description: Rogue certificate issuance from the Vault Intermediate CA. If a Vault policy misconfiguration allows a compromised service account (e.g., the Workflow Engine's Vault role) to request certificates with arbitrary Common Names (CN) or SANs beyond the expected service identity, the attacker can issue a certificate for 'keycloak.fedsecure.gov.internal' — impersonating the SSO provider within the cluster. The Istio mTLS STRICT mode relies on SPIFFE X.509-SVID validation; a forged certificate with the SPIFFE URI SAN of the Keycloak workload bypasses mTLS identity verification. This allows the attacker to intercept and replay Keycloak OIDC token responses, modifying JWT claims (e.g., elevating role from case_agent to admin) before forwarding to the API Gateway.
mitigations: SC-17: Restrict Vault PKI roles — each service's Vault role must only be allowed to issue certificates matching its SPIFFE URI SAN (spiffe://cluster.fedsecure.gov/ns/{namespace}/sa/{serviceaccount}); enforce 'allowed_uri_sans' in Vault PKI role policy
IA-5(1): Enable Vault PKI certificate transparency logging — all issued certificates are logged to an append-only audit trail with issuing entity's Vault token identity
SC-7: Configure Istio to use SPIFFE Trust Domain 'cluster.fedsecure.gov' with pinned root CA fingerprint — any certificate from a different issuer hierarchy is rejected regardless of chain validity
AU-2: Alert in Splunk when Vault issues a certificate with a CN or SAN that does not match the expected service identity for the requesting Vault role — automatic revocation trigger

AC-6(10): Apply Vault Sentinel policies — least-privilege constraint enforcement at policy evaluation time; prevent any single Vault token from generating certificates outside its assigned service namespace

threats.mitre_technique: T1499.002 - Endpoint Denial of Service: Service Exhaustion Flood
threats.description: A compromised application pod exploits RabbitMQ's lack of per-publisher rate limiting to flood a quorum queue with millions of messages per second, consuming all available memory on the RabbitMQ nodes. RabbitMQ 3.13 quorum queues store all messages in memory before persisting to disk — a message flood exhausts heap space, triggering the RabbitMQ memory alarm. When memory alarm fires, RabbitMQ blocks all publishing connections cluster-wide — including the Case Management Service's legitimate case lifecycle event publications and the Workflow Engine's approval triggers. This creates a Denial of Service against the CUI case management workflow, preventing government employees from processing case events during mission-critical operations. RabbitMQ CVE-2024-0234 (shovel/federation plugin authentication bypass) is also relevant as it allows unauthenticated federation establishment from a compromised node.
mitigations: SC-5: Configure RabbitMQ per-vhost and per-connection publish rate limits — restrict each service account to a maximum publish rate consistent with expected throughput (Case Management: 100 msg/s, Workflow Engine: 50 msg/s)
SC-5(1): Set explicit queue length limits (x-max-length) on all CUI-handling queues — implement overflow behavior 'reject-publish-dlx' to dead-letter excess messages without memory exhaustion

SI-2: Disable RabbitMQ shovel and federation plugins in the air-gap deployment — these plugins are not needed in the disconnected enclave and represent unnecessary attack surface (CVE-2024-0234 mitigation)
CM-6: Configure RabbitMQ memory high watermark at 0.4 (40% of available RAM) — alert via Prometheus/AlertManager at 30% before cluster-wide blocking occurs

IR-4: Implement application-level circuit breakers (Polly library in .NET services) — services degrade gracefully when RabbitMQ is unavailable; CUI workflows switch to synchronous mode rather than failing completely

threats.mitre_technique: T1558.001 - Steal or Forge Kerberos Tickets: Golden Ticket
threats.description: A Golden Ticket attack against the Agency Active Directory Kerberos infrastructure. If an attacker has compromised a Domain Controller (outside the FedRAMP boundary — within the agency's existing AD environment) and extracted the KRBTGT account hash, they can forge Kerberos TGTs for arbitrary domain accounts — including the Keycloak SCIM service account used for user provisioning. Keycloak federates identity from AD via LDAPS/Kerberos; a forged SCIM service account ticket allows the attacker to inject rogue user accounts with supervisor or admin roles directly into Keycloak via the SCIM provisioning endpoint, bypassing the normal CAC/PIV enrollment process. The injected account can then authenticate to the platform using a software credential (no smart card required) if the Keycloak SCIM endpoint does not enforce CAC/PIV for SCIM-provisioned accounts.
mitigations: IA-2(1): Enforce CAC/PIV authentication for ALL Keycloak user accounts regardless of provisioning source — SCIM-provisioned accounts must complete a CAC/PIV enrollment workflow before they are granted any role beyond 'pending_enrollment'

IA-8: Detect anomalous SCIM provisioning events — alert in Splunk when SCIM creates a user with a privileged role (supervisor, admin, audit_admin) without a corresponding CAC/PIV enrollment event within 24 hours
AC-2: Implement a provisioning approval workflow — any SCIM-provisioned account that arrives with a role beyond case_agent must be approved by an ISSM before activation
SC-8: Validate LDAPS certificate chain from the Agency AD LDAP server — reject connections if the AD server certificate does not chain to the agency's DoD PKI-issued intermediate CA
AU-2: Log all SCIM provisioning events (create, modify, delete) to the immutable Audit Service — correlate user creation events with HR system provisioning records via SIEM

threats.mitre_technique: T1612 - Build Image on Host
threats.description: Policy engine bypass via Kyverno admission webhook failure. In RKE2, the Kyverno validating admission webhook is configured with failurePolicy: Ignore by default during initial deployment. If the Kyverno controller pods are unavailable (e.g., due to an OOM-kill during a memory pressure event, or following a targeted DoS against Kyverno pods — TM-GOV-017 related), the Kubernetes API server fails open — allowing pod admission without policy enforcement. During the Kyverno outage window, an attacker (with kubectl access via a compromised CI/CD service account) can deploy a privileged pod (securityContext.privileged: true) or a pod with hostPID: true, achieving immediate container escape to host-level access without requiring CVE-exploitation (TM-GOV-004). The Kyverno policy gap is especially dangerous during cluster upgrade events when Kyverno may be temporarily unavailable.
mitigations: CM-6: Change Kyverno webhook failurePolicy to Fail for all enforcement-mode ClusterPolicies — accept partial cluster unavailability rather than fail-open on security policies

SC-7: Deploy Kyverno in high-availability mode (3+ replicas) with PodDisruptionBudget (maxUnavailable: 0 during normal operation) — ensure Kyverno cannot be OOM-killed without triggering PagerDuty alert

CM-7: Implement a backup Pod Security Admission (PSA) controller with restricted profile at the namespace level — PSA operates independently of Kyverno and enforces baseline security context restrictions even if Kyverno is down

AU-2: Monitor Kyverno webhook availability via Prometheus — alert within 60 seconds of webhook becoming unavailable; trigger automated incident response (halt CI/CD deployments until Kyverno restored)

AC-3: Restrict kubectl access to production namespaces — require Boundary PAM session recording for all kubectl commands; no CI/CD service account has kubectl exec or apply access to production without approval

threats.mitre_technique: T1213.003 - Data from Information Repositories: Code Repositories
threats.description: CUI data exfiltration via an XML External Entity (XXE) injection vulnerability in the Document Classification Service's document processing pipeline. The service uses .NET 8's XmlDocument class to parse uploaded XML-formatted case documents. If the XmlResolver is not explicitly disabled (XmlDocument.XmlResolver = null), a malicious uploaded document can include an XXE payload referencing internal system files (e.g., '<!ENTITY xxe SYSTEM "file:///etc/vault-token">') — extracting the Vault agent token from the container filesystem, or referencing internal Kubernetes service endpoints ('file:///var/run/secrets/kubernetes.io/serviceaccount/token') to steal the Kubernetes service account JWT. The ClamAV malware scan (C007) does not detect XXE payloads as malware — they are syntactically valid XML.
mitigations: SI-10: Disable XML external entity resolution in all .NET XmlDocument and XmlReader instances — explicitly set XmlDocument.XmlResolver = null and XmlReaderSettings.DtdProcessing = DtdProcessing.Prohibit
SI-3: Supplement ClamAV malware scan with a YARA rule set specifically targeting XXE and SSRF payload patterns in uploaded documents — scan before XmlDocument processing
CM-7: Use a strict document schema validator (XSD) before accepting XML documents for CUI classification — reject any document that does not conform to the approved CUI document schema
AC-6: Apply read-only filesystem to the Document Classification Service container — prevent XXE file disclosure via SYSTEM entity by ensuring the container cannot read sensitive host-mounted paths

RA-5: Include XXE testing in the annual FedRAMP penetration test scope — validate XmlResolver is disabled in all parsing code paths via static analysis (Semgrep rule for XmlDocument without XmlResolver=null)

threats.mitre_technique: T1070.002 - Indicator Removal: Clear Linux or Mac System Logs
threats.description: An advanced insider threat or APT actor who has gained Splunk admin access (via a compromised security operations user account) uses the Splunk REST API to suppress specific index searches and delete historical events from the _audit index. Splunk Enterprise does not provide WORM-immutable log storage by default — the _audit index can be deleted or rotated by a Splunk admin. This allows the attacker to erase evidence of their activities (CUI data access, privilege escalation, SIEM alert tuning changes that disabled detection rules) from the security operations record. Under FedRAMP ConMon requirements, this creates an AU-9 violation — the system can no longer prove audit record integrity to the 3PAO during annual assessment.
mitigations: AU-9: Enable Splunk SmartStore with Amazon S3-compatible backend (MinIO WORM bucket) — all indexed events are immediately frozen to WORM storage; Splunk admin cannot delete frozen data
AU-9(4): Segregate Splunk admin role from SOC analyst role — Splunk admin (infrastructure team) cannot search security-sensitive indexes; SOC analysts can search but not delete
AU-10: Forward all Splunk _internal and _audit logs to a separate out-of-band log store (e.g., a separate SIEM instance or a data diode to DISA ACAS) — independent record of Splunk admin activity
AC-5: Require two-person approval for any Splunk index deletion or retention policy change — change requires ISSM signature and is logged to the immutable Audit Service (C017)
IR-5: Conduct monthly integrity checks of Splunk index event counts — compare against the SIEM's known-good baseline to detect unauthorized event deletion

threats.mitre_technique: T1213 - Data from Information Repositories
threats.description: CUI leakage via OpenTelemetry trace span attributes. The OpenTelemetry Collector is configured with a PII/CUI scrubbing processor before export, but the scrubbing processor uses a keyword-based redaction approach that only matches explicit field names (e.g., 'user.ssn', 'case.cui_content'). Developers who add observability instrumentation directly to the Case Management Service may inadvertently include CUI values in span attributes using non-standard field names (e.g., db.statement containing a full SQL SELECT result with CUI case data, or http.response.body in debug traces). These unrecognized field names bypass the scrubbing processor and are exported to Jaeger (distributed tracing) — where Jaeger's access control grants read access to all developers with the 'ops-readonly' role, not just cleared personnel. This violates SC-28 and SI-12 data output handling requirements.
mitigations: SI-12: Replace keyword-based scrubbing with a deny-by-default approach — OTel Collector filter processor should DROP all span attributes not on an explicit allowlist of approved, non-CUI fields
AC-3: Restrict Jaeger UI access to personnel with IL5 clearance only — separate RBAC group 'security-ops-il5' required; 'ops-readonly' role must not have access to production trace data

CM-7: Prohibit OTel instrumentation of HTTP response bodies and DB query results in all .NET services — enforce via static analysis (Semgrep rule detecting AddTag calls with db.statement or http.response keys)
AU-2: Enable OTel Collector self-monitoring — log any span attribute that triggers a scrubbing processor match to the Splunk SIEM for developer remediation tracking
SC-8: Enforce TLS 1.3 on all OTLP gRPC endpoints between application services and the OTel Collector — ensure trace data cannot be intercepted in transit within the cluster

threats.mitre_technique: T1078.001 - Valid Accounts: Default Accounts
threats.mitre_sub_technique: T1021.001 - Remote Services: Remote Desktop Protocol
threats.description: Privilege escalation via Boundary credential brokering misconfiguration. Boundary 0.16's credential stores allow assignment of a credential library to a target — when an operator connects to the PostgreSQL target via Boundary, Boundary fetches a dynamic Vault credential with the role specified in the credential library. If the Vault dynamic secret role is misconfigured to use the PostgreSQL superuser role (postgres) rather than a least-privilege application-specific role, every Boundary session grants the operator full PostgreSQL superuser access — including the ability to DROP tables, modify RLS policies, and create new database users. A rogue operator (insider threat) could use this to create a persistent backdoor database user that persists across Boundary session expiry, providing ongoing unauthorized access to CUI data outside of the Boundary audit trail.
mitigations: AC-6: Audit all Vault dynamic secret roles for PostgreSQL — verify that Boundary credential libraries reference least-privilege roles (e.g., case_reader, case_writer) never the postgres superuser
AC-2(3): Implement Vault dynamic secret role validation — Vault Sentinel policy prevents creation of any dynamic secret role with PostgreSQL superuser or CREATEUSER privileges
AU-14: Enable Boundary session recording for ALL database sessions — recordings stored in MinIO WORM bucket, reviewed by ISSO monthly; alert on sessions exceeding expected duration (> 30 min)

SI-4: Enable pgaudit to log all CREATE USER, ALTER ROLE, and GRANT statements — forward to Splunk SIEM; alert on any new database user creation that does not correspond to a Vault dynamic secret issuance event
AC-17: Enforce maximum Boundary session duration of 30 minutes for database targets — require re-authentication (new Boundary session with new Vault credential) for extended maintenance windows

threats.mitre_technique: T1562.010 - Impair Defenses: Downgrade Attack
threats.description: An insider threat (ISSO or system administrator with access to the OpenSCAP runner) manipulates SCAP scan results before they are exported to OSCAL Assessment Results format. The attack modifies the XCCDF result XML file on the scanning node after the scan completes but before the OSCAL CLI converts it to an OSCAL Assessment Results artifact. By changing 'fail' rule results to 'pass' in the XCCDF output, the attacker produces a fraudulent OSCAL Assessment Results document that shows full STIG compliance — used as FedRAMP ConMon evidence submitted to the 3PAO. This allows STIG violations (e.g., disabled FIPS mode, insecure SSH cipher suites, missing ClamAV signatures) to remain undetected and unreported in the POA&M. The OSCAL artifacts are not cryptographically signed, making forgery undetectable without independent re-scanning.
mitigations: AU-10: Sign all OSCAL Assessment Results artifacts with the Vault transit RSA-3072 signing key immediately after oscal-cli conversion — 3PAO can verify signature against the public key published in the SSP

AU-9(3): Implement a separate, automated scan pipeline that runs OpenSCAP without operator access to intermediate XCCDF files — results pipeline: RHEL 9 node -> OpenSCAP -> XCCDF (root-only tmpfs) -> oscal-cli -> MinIO WORM (signed OSCAL)
AU-9: Store XCCDF intermediate results in a read-only tmpfs mount that is automatically deleted after OSCAL conversion — no operator can access the intermediate file
CM-6: Enable automated re-scanning as an independent verification step — a second OpenSCAP scan run by the ISSO (separate account) must produce results within 5% variance of the automated scan
RA-5(6): Engage the 3PAO to perform independent SCAP scans quarterly — compare 3PAO results against the system-generated OSCAL artifacts; flag discrepancies as material for POA&M

threats.mitre_technique: T1068 - Exploitation for Privilege Escalation
threats.mitre_sub_technique: T1547.006 - Boot or Logon Autostart Execution: Kernel Modules and Extensions
threats.description: A kernel privilege escalation exploit targeting the Linux kernel's nf_tables netfilter subsystem (CVE-2024-1086 — use-after-free in nft_verdict_init) gives an unprivileged local user (e.g., a container process that has escaped to the host via TM-GOV-004) full root privileges on the RHEL 9 node. The exploit bypasses the node's FIPS-mode kernel hardening because nf_tables is part of the network subsystem, not the cryptographic subsystem — FIPS mode does not disable or harden nf_tables. On a compromised node, the attacker gains full control of all pod secrets (Vault tokens, database credentials, SPIFFE private keys), the kubelet certificate (allowing impersonation of the node to the Kubernetes API server), and can install a kernel module rootkit that persists across pod restarts. This is the highest-impact threat in this model — a single exploited container workload results in full IL5 node compromise.
mitigations: SI-2(2): Apply RHEL 9 kernel patch immediately — CVE-2024-1086 is patched in kernel-5.14.0-362.8.1.el9_3; validate via 'rpm -q kernel' on all nodes; treat as P0 emergency patch per FedRAMP continuous monitoring requirements
CM-7: Disable nf_tables kernel module on nodes where it is not required for cluster networking — use RKE2's CIS-hardened profile which restricts loadable kernel modules to an approved list
SI-7(6): Deploy Falco rule detecting nft_verdict_init exploit chain indicators: unusual privilege escalation from container UID to UID 0 within the same kernel namespace without execve transition
CM-6: Enable RHEL 9 kernel lockdown mode (lockdown=integrity) — prevents loading of unsigned kernel modules even by root, mitigating post-exploitation persistence via kernel module rootkits

RA-5(1): Subscribe to US-CERT/CISA KEV (Known Exploited Vulnerabilities) feed — CVE-2024-1086 is on the CISA KEV list with SSVC priority: Act; apply within 15 calendar days per FedRAMP High ConMon requirements

threats.mitre_technique: T1562.001 - Impair Defenses: Disable or Modify Tools
threats.description: An attacker who has gained container escape (TM-GOV-004) or root on a node (TM-GOV-025) kills the Falco eBPF probe by sending SIGKILL to the falco-driver process, disabling all runtime syscall monitoring on that node for the remainder of the attack. Falco's Sidekick alerting is based on Falco sending events — if the Falco process is killed, the Sidekick receives no events and produces no alerts, creating a silent blind spot. During the window of Falco unavailability, the attacker can perform CUI exfiltration, privilege escalation, and lateral movement without generating any SIEM alerts. Falco 0.38 does not have self-protection mechanisms against process termination by root. The Falco systemd unit on the RHEL 9 node does automatically restart, but there is a 30-60 second window of blindness during the restart cycle.
mitigations: SI-4: Configure Splunk SIEM correlation rule detecting Falco heartbeat loss — Falco Sidekick sends a health event every 30 seconds; if no event for 60 seconds from a specific node, trigger P1 alert

SC-7: Deploy a secondary, independent host-based monitoring agent (e.g., RHEL auditd with audisp-remote) running independently of Falco — auditd cannot be killed by the same privilege level as the attacking process
SI-7(7): Configure Falco systemd unit with StartLimitBurst=10 and RestartSec=5 — ensure rapid restart; add systemd watchdog (WatchdogSec=30) to detect and restart hung Falco processes

CM-7: Apply Linux immutable file attribute to Falco binary (chattr +i /usr/bin/falco) and use SELinux confined process domain — prevent replacement or modification of the Falco binary by a compromised root process

IR-4: Document and test Falco outage incident response playbook — ISSO must be notified within 5 minutes of Falco monitoring loss on any IL5 node; cluster-wide network isolation may be required until Falco is restored

threats.mitre_technique: T1048.002 - Exfiltration Over Alternative Protocol: Exfiltration Over Asymmetric Encrypted Non-C2 Protocol
threats.description: CUI data exfiltration via the email notification channel. The Notification Service is designed to send reference links (not CUI content) per SI-12. However, a compromised Workflow Engine that can trigger arbitrary notification events may craft a notification with a template that extracts CUI from the Case Management Service's response context and embeds it in the email body — violating SI-12 information output handling. The DISA-approved SMTP relay does not perform content inspection for CUI (it is a transport relay, not a DLP gateway). Emails with embedded CUI are delivered to .gov recipients, but if an email account is compromised or forwarded to an external address, the CUI leaves the authorization boundary via a permitted channel (SMTP). This is a covert exfiltration path that does not trigger Falco network egress alerts (SMTP is permitted).
mitigations: SI-12: Enforce strict template validation in the Notification Service — email templates must be parsed and validated against an approved schema; any template containing database field references beyond case_id and case_url must be rejected

AC-3: The Notification Service must not have direct access to the Case Management Service or PostgreSQL — it receives only case_id and escalation_type from the Workflow Engine via RabbitMQ; it must call a dedicated notification-context endpoint that returns only non-CUI safe fields

SI-7: Implement a DLP gateway at the SMTP relay — deploy a CUI keyword scanner (based on the NARA CUI category word lists) that quarantines outbound emails containing CUI pattern matches for ISSO review

AU-2: Log all outbound email metadata (recipient, subject hash, template name, triggering workflow) to the immutable Audit Service — detect anomalous notification volumes (> 100 emails/hour from a single workflow) in Splunk

CM-7: Implement allow-listing for notification recipients — the Notification Service may only send to email addresses in the agency's .mil/.gov domain; non-government recipient addresses must trigger an immediate alert and email hold

threats.mitre_technique: T1552.007 - Unsecured Credentials: Container API
threats.description: CUI aggregation leakage through improperly sanitized analytics aggregation queries. The Analytics Service provides reports (case volume by agency, workflow cycle times, CUI category distribution) for government program managers. A user with the analytics_viewer role (not necessarily cleared at IL5) can craft analytics query parameters that effectively reconstruct individual CUI case records through a series of narrowly-scoped aggregation queries — an 'aggregation attack'. For example, filtering by agency + case_type + date_range + status = single case_id progressively narrows results until a single record is identifiable. The analytics service performs no minimum aggregation count enforcement (k-anonymity threshold), allowing de-anonymization of CUI case data through repeated differential queries. This violates AC-3 data access controls and SI-12 output sanitization requirements.
mitigations: AC-3: Implement k-anonymity enforcement in the Analytics Service — any aggregation query returning fewer than k=5 distinct records must return a suppressed result ('< 5 cases') rather than the actual value
AC-6: Restrict analytics_viewer role to pre-defined, approved dashboard queries only — prevent ad-hoc SQL or filter parameter combinations; enforce query allowlist validation server-side

AU-2: Log all analytics queries with full filter parameter set and result cardinality to the Audit Service — SIEM correlation rule detecting repeated queries that progressively narrow the result set (potential aggregation attack pattern)
AC-3(13): Implement differential privacy noise injection (Laplace mechanism) for numeric analytics outputs — add calibrated noise to aggregated counts so individual records cannot be reconstructed

SC-8: The analytics service must query the read replica (C022) with a service account that has SELECT access only on aggregate-safe views (no access to CUI raw columns) — enforce via PostgreSQL view-level RLS

risk_summary.assessor_risk_opinion: This platform demonstrates strong baseline security controls (Istio mTLS, Vault HSM, STIG-hardened RHEL 9, CAC/PIV) consistent with a mature FedRAMP High authorization effort. However, four CRITICAL findings (TM-GOV-001, TM-GOV-003, TM-GOV-004, TM-GOV-025) represent exploitable vulnerabilities in the authentication, cryptographic, and container runtime layers that must be resolved before ATO issuance. The Leaky Vessels kernel CVEs (TM-GOV-004) and nf_tables privilege escalation (TM-GOV-025) are on the CISA KEV list and constitute immediate patching requirements under FedRAMP ConMon SLAs. The non-FIPS AES-ECB usage (TM-GOV-003) is a direct FIPS 140-3 compliance violation that will result in a Condition on ATO unless remediated before the 3PAO assessment. The missing Istio default-deny policy (TM-GOV-005) and Kyverno fail-open misconfiguration (TM-GOV-019) represent systemic architectural gaps that elevate the overall risk posture from MEDIUM to HIGH.
